content.text: An MCP transport is the communication channel between a host (or client) and a server. The protocol is always JSON-RPC 2.0, but the medium varies. MCP defines two standard transports:
, where the server is a subprocess communicating over standard input/output, and
, where a remote server accepts POST requests and streams responses via Server-Sent Events. Choosing the right transport determines deployment topology, scalability, and authentication requirements.

attrs.alt: stdio vs HTTP+SSE transport comparison: flow diagrams, characteristics, and best-for guidance

content.text: The transport is transparent to your tool logic. Switch from stdio to HTTP+SSE and the tool handler code does not change — only the server startup and client connection code differs.
In the stdio transport, the MCP client spawns the server as a child process. Each JSON-RPC message is a single line of JSON terminated by
. The client writes to the server’s stdin, the server writes to its stdout. Stderr is left for logging and never carries protocol traffic.
One client per server process — stdio is a point-to-point channel.
The server process dies when the client closes the pipe; no explicit teardown needed.
No authentication surface: process ownership is the security boundary.
Latency is near-zero (inter-process memory copy), ideal for local tools.
object specifying the command to launch.

import asyncio
from mcp import ClientSession, StdioServerParameters
from mcp.client.stdio import stdio_client

server_params = StdioServerParameters(
    command="python",
    args=["file_server.py"],
    env={"HOME": "/home/user"},   # restrict env variables
)

async def main():
    async with stdio_client(server_params) as (read, write):
        async with ClientSession(read, write) as session:
            await session.initialize()
            tools = await session.list_tools()
            print("Tools:", [t.name for t in tools.tools])
            result = await session.call_tool("read_file", {"path": "/etc/hostname"})
            print(result.content[0].text)

asyncio.run(main())
The HTTP+SSE transport runs the server as a long-lived HTTP service. The client sends JSON-RPC requests as HTTP POST requests; the server pushes responses and notifications back over a persistent
(SSE) connection. A single server can serve multiple clients concurrently.
connection first — the server keeps this stream open for the session lifetime.
All client requests go as POST to
(or whatever path the server declares); the session ID ties the POST to the SSE channel.
Multiple clients share one server process — horizontal scaling is straightforward.
Auth headers (Bearer tokens, API keys) can be validated on the POST endpoint.
Works across network boundaries, containers, and cloud deployments.
handles SSE bookkeeping; you only provide tool logic.

import asyncio
from starlette.applications import Starlette
from starlette.routing import Route
import uvicorn
from mcp.server import Server
from mcp.server.sse import SseServerTransport
from mcp.types import Tool, TextContent
import httpx

app = Server("web-search-server")

@app.list_tools()
async def list_tools():
    return [
        Tool(
            name="web_search",
            description="Search the web using DuckDuckGo",
            inputSchema={
                "type": "object",
                "properties": {"query": {"type": "string"}},
                "required": ["query"],
            },
        )
    ]

@app.call_tool()
async def call_tool(name: str, arguments: dict):
    if name == "web_search":
        url = "https://api.duckduckgo.com/"
        params = {"q": arguments["query"], "format": "json", "no_html": "1"}
        async with httpx.AsyncClient() as client:
            r = await client.get(url, params=params)
        data = r.json()
        abstract = data.get("Abstract", "No abstract available")
        return [TextContent(type="text", text=abstract)]
    raise ValueError(f"Unknown tool: {name}")

sse = SseServerTransport("/message")

async def handle_sse(request):
    async with sse.connect_sse(request.scope, request.receive, request._send) as (r, w):
        await app.run(r, w, app.create_initialization_options())

starlette_app = Starlette(
    routes=[
        Route("/sse", endpoint=handle_sse),
        Route("/message", endpoint=sse.handle_post_message, methods=["POST"]),
    ]
)

if __name__ == "__main__":
    uvicorn.run(starlette_app, host="0.0.0.0", port=8000)
Connecting as an HTTP + SSE Client
with the server base URL. The client automatically opens the SSE stream and routes responses.

import asyncio
from mcp import ClientSession
from mcp.client.sse import sse_client

async def main():
    async with sse_client("http://localhost:8000/sse") as (read, write):
        async with ClientSession(read, write) as session:
            await session.initialize()
            result = await session.call_tool(
                "web_search", {"query": "model context protocol"}
            )
            print(result.content[0].text)

asyncio.run(main())
To pass authentication credentials, pass headers to

async with sse_client(
    "https://api.example.com/mcp/sse",
    headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {os.environ['MCP_TOKEN']}"},
) as (read, write):
    async with ClientSession(read, write) as session:
        await session.initialize()
        ...
Choosing Between stdio and HTTP + SSE
Both transports fully implement MCP. The decision is about deployment topology and operational requirements.
Dev tools, IDE plugins, CLI agents
Shared services, SaaS integrations, multi-tenant agents
Regardless of transport, every MCP message is a JSON-RPC 2.0 object. Understanding the wire format helps when debugging or implementing a custom transport.
Each message is one JSON object on a single line, followed by
. No length prefix, no binary framing.
The client POSTs the JSON-RPC message; the server pushes the response as an SSE
event on the open SSE stream.

# Step 1 — client opens SSE stream
GET /sse HTTP/1.1
Host: localhost:8000
Accept: text/event-stream

# Server responds (kept open):
HTTP/1.1 200 OK
Content-Type: text/event-stream
event: endpoint
data: /message?sessionId=abc123

# Step 2 — client sends request
POST /message?sessionId=abc123 HTTP/1.1
Content-Type: application/json

{"jsonrpc":"2.0","id":1,"method":"tools/call","params":{...}}

# Step 3 — server pushes response on the SSE stream
event: message
data: {"jsonrpc":"2.0","id":1,"result":{...}}
Most tool logic should be tested without a live transport. The MCP SDK provides
for in-process integration testing — no subprocess or network required.

# pytest test for the file_server stdio transport
import pytest
from mcp import ClientSession, StdioServerParameters
from mcp.client.stdio import stdio_client
import subprocess, sys

@pytest.mark.asyncio
async def test_read_file_tool(tmp_path):
    # Write a temp file the server can read
    f = tmp_path / "hello.txt"
    f.write_text("hello world")

    params = StdioServerParameters(command=sys.executable, args=["file_server.py"])
    async with stdio_client(params) as (r, w):
        async with ClientSession(r, w) as session:
            await session.initialize()
            result = await session.call_tool("read_file", {"path": str(f)})
            assert result.isError is False
            assert result.content[0].text == "hello world"

@pytest.mark.asyncio
async def test_read_file_path_traversal():
    params = StdioServerParameters(command=sys.executable, args=["file_server.py"])
    async with stdio_client(params) as (r, w):
        async with ClientSession(r, w) as session:
            await session.initialize()
            result = await session.call_tool("read_file", {"path": "../etc/passwd"})
            assert "traversal" in result.content[0].text.lower()
to run an in-process server during tests.
The host (not the server) controls the process lifecycle. If the host exits, the server exits too.
Restart logic lives in the host: if the server crashes, re-spawn it and re-initialize.
Avoid writing large logs to stdout — it corrupts the JSON-RPC stream. Always log to stderr or a log file.
key) restricts which environment variables reach the subprocess.
Run behind a reverse proxy (nginx, Caddy) for TLS termination and rate limiting.
SSE connections are long-lived — configure proxy timeout to match your maximum session duration (e.g.
Scale horizontally with sticky sessions: route each
to the same backend instance, or use a Redis-backed session store.
before opening the SSE stream to fail fast on invalid tokens.
runs the server as a subprocess: one client, near-zero latency, zero auth setup — ideal for local developer tools and IDE integrations.
runs the server as an ASGI service: many clients, network-accessible, supports auth headers — ideal for shared APIs and cloud deployments.
The JSON-RPC 2.0 messages are identical on both transports; switching transports requires only changing the server startup and client connection code.
on the client side for stdio and
Never write protocol traffic to stdout in a stdio server; log only to stderr or a log file.
